next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

The call came at 11:47 PM. Mallick was on the couch watching the ten o'clock weather — a front pushing in from the Dakotas, rain by Wednesday — and thinking about whether the tomato cages would hold. She picked up on the second ring.

Dispatch gave her the address and she knew it before the numbers finished. Martin Kessler's lake house. Elbow Lake. Second call to the same property in four days.

She wrote it down anyway. She always wrote it down.

She changed out of sweats. Blazer, polo, slacks, the low boots she wore for outdoor scenes. She clipped her reading glasses to the chain around her neck, took her leather notebook from the kitchen counter, and left through the garage. The casserole dish was in the cabinet above the stove. She did not take it.

The drive from Detroit Lakes took twenty-six minutes at that hour. No traffic on County Road 6, just her headlights cutting through the pines and the occasional pair of deer eyes flashing green at the tree line. She drove with the window cracked. September cold at this hour — low forties, the kind that reminded you October was two weeks away. She didn't turn on the radio.

She thought about Saturday. The green bean casserole. Getting Kessler's name wrong — Kessner, she'd called him, three times — and the way they'd all fallen over themselves to correct her. Paul Decker with his careful insurance-man precision. Cliff Haywood with his smooth real-estate courtesy. Each correction a gift she'd cataloged and filed.

Saturday was a different job. Saturday she'd been working a suspicious death that smelled like natura...
open_hooks_to_carry: The investigation into Martin's death winding down — Mallick leaning toward natural causes
Doug goes to the dock for his evening ritual — the same dock from his quiet moment in Ch 7
Doug finds Cliff floating face-down in the water beside the dock
The details that signal staging: Cliff's leather loafers instead of his deck shoes, the gash on his temple, shallow water
Doug's scream — the quietest man in the group makes the sound that ends the comedy
The doubled grief: Cliff's death and the death of the weekend as something survivable
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ith his duffel bag and his podcast mic and his phone with Mia's last text — *Miss you. Come home safe* — still glowing on the screen. He brushed his teeth with the bathroom door open because the basement bathroom was small and the ventilation was bad and Martin had always said he'd fix the fan and never had. He set his alarm. He lay in the dark and listened to the old refrigerator kick on, its compressor clunking into its forty-minute rhythm, and the house above him settling with the sounds old houses make — pipes, wood, wind, or the weight of six men trying to sleep in a house that still belonged to the seventh.

He thought about Tommy's hands on the railing and Cliff three chairs away and Doug's water and Joel looking at Paul looking at Joel, and he thought: *something is wrong and I don't know what it is and I'm the guy who's supposed to know what it is.*

He filed the thought. He'd come back to it. He'd come back to all of it — the punt return yards and the draft board and Martin's steak and the loons and the lake and the sound of six men laughing at the same time for the last time.

He didn't know it was the last time. But his body knew. His body had been keeping score all weekend, and the score was bad, and the clock was running, and nobody had called the timeout.

He slept. Eventually, they all did. It was the last night all of them would sleep in this house, but nobody knew that yet, and the house held them the way it always had — old wood, lake damp, the pine smell and the dark water and the dock reaching out into the lake like a question that hadn't been answered.
secondary_character_beats: Doug's discovery should draw on the vulnerability established in Ch 7 — this is the man who came here because he needed people, and now he's finding a dead one
The group's arrival at the dock should show each man's specific shock — not a generic reaction
state_deltas_required: the second death — the real murder
Cliff is dead. The comedy is over. Everything the reader laughed at in chapters 1-13 is reframed — the clues to the murder were in the comedy.
